observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the LCE, through the OIC-City Accountant, to adhere to the procedures for the proper disposition and derecognition of dormant accounts pursuant to Items 7.0 and 8.0 of COA Circular No. 2023-008, thus:

Secure all documents available relative to the said accounts such as accounting records, reports, vouchers, and its supporting documents.

Prepare the following schedules quarterly and submit the same on the 15th day of the ensuing month:
Schedule of Dormant Receivable Accounts
Schedule of Dormant Unliquidated Cash Advances 
Schedule of Dormant Intra/Inter-agency Fund Transfers
Schedule of Other Dormant Accounts

For accounts with records and documents or when original documents are lost and destroyed, or cannot be produced, the City Accountant shall:

Prepare letters signed by the LCE addressed to each accountable officer concerned demanding the AO to execute a certificate of justification as to why the supporting documents are not available along with the narration or documentation of Management’s effort to locate such documents. In case of retired, deceased and separated AOs, the current AO shall execute the certificate of justification.

Prepare a (1) list of available records relative to the dormant account and indicate the extent of validation made thereof along with the documents itself. Include also a (2) list of lost documents or records.

Submit a detailed report together with the said documents/reports to the LCE recommending the conduct of investigation to determine the cause/s of the loss of documents and pinpoint the liable official/employee for such loss.

Further, we recommended to the LCE to:

Create an Investigation Committee to determine the cause/s of the loss or destruction of documents, pinpoint the liable official/employee, and look into the absence or presence of negligence on the part of AO through submission of an Investigation Report (IR).

Render decision-based on the IR. A copy of the IR shall be submitted to the City Accountant.

Require the City Accountant to submit a request, through the LCE, for the approval of COA to write off/derecognize from the books the dormant accounts.

Moreover, we recommended that the LCE direct the (a) City Treasurer to transfer the deposited collections from the previous years from the General Fund account to the Trust Fund account with a total amount of ₱1,864,802.96 and furnish the proof of transfer to the City Accountant. 

(b) The City Accountant shall then prepare the necessary adjusting entries upon transfer.

observations.recommendation: To address these financial misstatements, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the City Accountant to ensure compliance with the provisions of Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 and IPSAS 1. The City Accountant to take appropriate steps to implement the following adjusting entries to address the observations noted by the Audit Team, to wit:

We recommended that the LCE take the following actions:
Designate appropriate storage areas for inventories managed by the Acting Head Supply Officer, including office supplies, janitorial supplies, and other materials, to ensure proper safekeeping, monitoring, and accountability.
Direct the Acting Head Supply Officer to:
Conduct a physical count of all inventories as of year-end and prepare the corresponding RPCI, in compliance with the provisions of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
Ensure the preparation and use of Requisition and Issuance Slips (RIS) for all issued or distributed items, which shall then be summarized in the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to support inventory monitoring and reporting.
Instruct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to recognize the expenses related to the issuance of inventories, duly supported by the corresponding RIS and SSMI, in order to adjust the year-end inventory balances and ensure accurate and reliable financial reporting.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:
Derecognize inventories that were delivered and received in CY 2025 by recording the following adjusting entry, in accordance with IPSAS 12, to reflect the correct period of recognition:

Ensure that only inventories delivered and accepted within the reporting period are recorded in the books at year-end, supported by valid IARs and other relevant documentation, thereby improving the accuracy and reliability of financial reporting.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:
Ensure proper documentation of all distribution activities by coordinating with concerned offices to submit the following:
Duly signed lists containing the individual names of recipients or beneficiaries of the distributed rice stockpiles and knapsack sprayers, to establish accountability and validate the legitimacy of the distribution.
Program proposals outlining the objectives, target beneficiaries, distribution schedules, quantity of goods, and selection criteria, to ensure transparency and accountability in the implementation of distribution programs.
Require offices to submit geotagged photos of distribution activities using GPS-enabled cameras or mobile applications available from the App Store or Google Play Store, to ensure verifiable location and time data that support the authenticity and validity of reported distributions.
Record the following journal entries to recognize the issuance of goods and correct the Inventory account balance:

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Inventory Committee to prioritize and expedite the preparation and submission of the RPCPPE for CY 2024 to the Audit Team, Supply Office, and Accounting Office to facilitate the reconciliation and recording in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006.
Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to record the following adjusting journal entries to correct the PPE account presented in the financial statements:

Based on the above observations, we recommended that the LCE establish a Disposal Committee, in accordance with the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties, responsible for inspecting, appraising, and conducting valuation activities, as well as overseeing the proper disposal of government properties, including the disposal of PPEs through transfer to other government agencies.

We recommended that the LCE require the City Accountant to:
On the Inadequate Disclosures in the Notes to the Financial Statement
Coordinate with other department heads to gather information on the deficiencies noted by the audit team, including non-financial data that is necessary for users of the financial statements to understand the financial operations of the City.
Provide adequate disclosures and properly present the same in the Notes to the Financial Statements based on the information provided by the concerned Department Heads.
On the Change in Accounting Policy on Semi-Expendable Properties
Ensure full compliance with IPSAS 3 by applying changes in accounting policies retrospectively. The prior year’s financial statements should be restated to reflect the recognition of semi-expendable inventories and related expenses, thereby enhancing the comparability and fair presentation of financial information.
Additionally, proper disclosures must be made in the Notes to the Financial Statements to explain the nature and impact of the change.
On the Inconsistencies in SCBAA, including reconciliation with SFPer
Ensure accurate reconciliation between the SCBAA and the SFPer to enhance the reliability, transparency, and overall usefulness of both financial reports, in compliance with IPSAS, by:
Addressing the deficiencies noted by the Audit Team;
Verifying the accuracy of reported reconciling amounts; and
Providing a detailed breakdown of reconciling items in the reconciliation statement disclosed in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to:
Prepare and present separate financial reports for each special account within the GF, in accordance with Section 110 of the Manual on NGAS, Volume I.
Maintain subsidiary ledgers for each special account in the GF to ensure complete and accurate recording.
Ensure proper classification through separate presentation of loan proceeds distinct from the 20% DF to enhance the accuracy of financial reporting.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the LCE direct the City Budget Officer to undertake the following corrective measures:
Ensure the preparation and issuance of AROs prior to the incurrence of obligations. AROs must be duly approved by the LCE, in compliance with the provisions of the BOM for LGUs.
Discontinue the practice of equating appropriations with allotments unless these are backed by actual cash or confirmed revenue collections. The release of allotments should be strictly based on validated fund availability to avoid overcommitment.
Establish regular coordination with the City Accountant to reconcile the RAAO with the accounting records to ensure accuracy and integrity of financial reporting.
Exclude unreleased loan proceeds from the computation of available funds when determining allotments. Allotments should be issued only upon actual receipt of the loan proceeds or upon formal confirmation of disbursement schedules specific to the current year from the lending institutions to ensure that funding sources are reliable and prevent the issuance of allotments without actual or assured cash backing.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Treasurer to implement the following actions:
Through the Designated Liquidating Officer:
Immediately deposit the outstanding balance of ₱2,331,763.85 into the AGDB and provide the validated deposit slip to the Audit Team for verification.
Ensure that all daily collections are deposited intact on the next banking day in compliance with Section 32 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
Collections scheduled for deposit on the next banking day shall be securely stored in the vault to ensure their safety and prevent unauthorized access.
Perform end-of-day cash-on-hand reconciliations to ensure accuracy and prevent under-deposits, over-deposits, or shortages before depositing on the next banking day.
Strictly monitor daily remittances of collections to the Designated Liquidating Officer or assign a responsible officer to oversee the process, ensuring all funds are deposited promptly on the next banking day.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Treasurer to implement the following measures:
Through the Assistant City Treasurer, as the officer in charge of the fidelity bond application:
Conduct a thorough review of the cash accountability assigned to each accountable officer to ensure alignment with actual collection trends.
Adjust the maximum cash accountability limits of bonded accountable officers who regularly handle large collections, following the procedures outlined in Paragraph 7.2 of Treasury Circular No. 02-2019.
While the approval of the increased fidelity bond is in process, any large collections should be handled by another accountable officer with sufficient cash accountability and immediately remitted to the Liquidating Officer.
Require the Liquidating Officer to furnish a copy of the daily collections and closely monitor compliance with remittance requirements, reporting any non-compliance for immediate action.
Issue notifications to each accountable officer to inform them of their maximum cash accountability limits.
Require all accountable officers, including those assigned in field offices, to remit their daily collections to the Liquidating Officer for immediate deposit into the AGDB.
If daily remittance is not possible, establish a structured remittance schedule that ensures timely deposits at consistent intervals—such as every two or three days—based on the volume of collections and operational practicality.
Issue a memorandum or directive to all accountable officers reiterating the mandatory daily remittance policy.
If no collections are made for the day, the accountable officer must inform the Liquidating Officer for proper documentation and reporting.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Treasurer to:
Prepare and submit a request for the disposal of the obsolete unused checks, along with the corresponding accountable forms, to the Audit Team for proper evaluation and disposition in accordance with prescribed guidelines under Section 99 of GAAM, Volume I.
Prepare a report requesting relief from accountability for the concerned accountable officers or the City Treasurer, detailing the results of the investigation, the cause of the loss, and the justification for granting the request, in compliance with Section 54 of the Manual on the NGAS, Volume I.
Require all accountable officers to strictly comply with Section 50 of the Manual on the NGAS, Volume II, by preparing and submitting Individual RAAFs at the end of each month for consolidation.
Ensure the regular submission of the CRAAF to the Audit Team in compliance with Section 51 of the Manual on the NGAS, Volume II, stating that the consolidated report, along with the individual reports, must be submitted to the Audit Team for verification no later than the 5th day of the following month.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Treasurer to:
Assign a different accountable officer as the Petty Cash Custodian to handle the revolving fund, ensuring that no single person has control over both collections and disbursements
Secure or increase the fidelity bond of the assigned accountable officer, if not yet bonded, or adjust it to sufficiently cover the maximum cash accountability of the revolving fund to safeguard public funds.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Acting CDRRMO, in coordination with the CPDC, City Budget Officer and City Accountant, to ensure strict compliance with the approved budget and the LDRRMFIP by:
Conducting thorough planning and prioritization of PPAs during the budget preparation process to ensure appropriate fund allocation, in accordance with Section 4 (1) of PD No. 1445, which requires that no money shall be paid out of any public treasury except in pursuance of an appropriation law;
Refraining from charging any expenditure to the LDRRMF that has not been duly appropriated and included in the approved LDRRMFIP; and
Avoiding the creation of budgetary appropriations without the support of a duly enacted appropriation ordinance, in line with sound fiscal management and legal budget practices.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Acting CDRRMO, in coordination with the CPDC and the City Budget Officer, to ensure the proper preparation and updating of the LDRRMFIP, incorporating the required 30% allocation for the QRF and a detailed listing of all PPAs to be funded from the prior years’ unexpended DRRMF under the Special Trust Fund pursuant with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the LCE direct the following officers to strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 by undertaking the following actions:
City Budget Officer/Personnel-in-charge of RAAO:
Adopt and implement the prescribed revised coding system for the RAAO specific to the LDRRMF to ensure proper classification, tracking, and monitoring of DRRM-related expenditures, in line with Paragraph 5.1.1; and
Establish and maintain separate RAAOs for the QRF, MOOE, and CO under the LDRRMF, as mandated by Paragraph 5.1.4, to eliminate risk of fund misclassification.
City Accountant, in coordination with the Acting CDRRMO, through the CDRRMC and LDC:
Prepare and regularly submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF to the Audit Team as required under Paragraph 5.1.5, to uphold transparency, accountability, and timely evaluation of fund utilization.
We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant make the necessary correcting entry to properly reflect the Trust Liability balances in the appropriate funds, as follows:

We recommended that the LCE instructs the following officials:
On the non-presentation of projects funded through loans in the AIP
CPDC, in coordination with the City Budget Officer, to ensure that all development projects financed through loans, particularly those with repayments charged against the 20% Development Fund, are explicitly identified and detailed in the AIP to promote transparency, ensure compliance with the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 and support effective planning, budgeting, and monitoring of fund utilization.
On the 71.43% implementation rate during CY 2024
Local Development Council (LDC), through the CPDC:
To strengthen project planning and preparation processes to ensure that only procurement- and implementation-ready projects are included in the AIP. Close coordination among the planning, engineering, and budget offices should be enforced to address bottlenecks in project execution.
To conduct monitoring and evaluation regularly to ensure timely implementation of all programmed development projects in accordance with DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1.
On the incomplete documentation
City Engineer, in coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), to provide the following supporting documents on the paving of asphalt overlay pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001:
Technical specifications for the original scope
Statement of Time Elapsed; and
PERT/CPM network diagram (and any revisions)
City Accountant to institute control, such as implementing documentary requirement checklist, to ensure that all required supporting documents are complete and attached prior to processing payments, especially for projects charged against the 20% DF; to prevent similar deficiencies and ensure transparency, accountability, and compliance with applicable laws and regulations.

We recommended that the City Government of Urdaneta, under the direction of the LCE and in coordination with MSWMC, promptly undertake a comprehensive review of the waste disposal operations at the UCESL. This review should include a thorough reassessment of the landfill's spatial layout, with particular attention to the proximity of existing cells to the Sinocalan River in compliance with RA No. 9003 (Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000), including the strict enforcement of proper waste segregation protocols which help prevent the accumulation of unsegregated waste and mitigate potential health risks to surrounding residents.

To address the identified deficiencies and strengthen internal controls over the assessment and collection of business taxes, we recommended that the LCE direct the BPLO to implement the following measures:
Intensify efforts to collect at least ₱1,964,625.36 in unpaid business taxes from certain establishments that have engaged in transactions with the City.
Coordinate closely with the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to obtain a complete and regularly updated list of payments made to suppliers and contractors. This data should be used as a reference point when evaluating business permit applications and declared gross receipts, serving as a minimum benchmark in tax assessment. Any declarations falling below this benchmark should be flagged for further verification prior to the issuance of business permits.
Develop and implement appropriate sanctions for businesses found to have under-declared gross receipts or submitted misleading information. Possible sanctions may include the imposition of penalties, surcharges, or suspension/revocation of business permits, in accordance with applicable laws and local ordinances.
Conduct training sessions and capacity-building workshops for BPLO personnel, with a focus on the proper interpretation and application of the City’s Local Revenue Code, particularly on tax rate schedules, classification criteria, and accurate assessment procedures, especially for newly hired or reassigned staff.
In addition, we recommended that Management, subject to budget availability, prioritize the enhancement of the in-house developed BPLO system to include the following functionalities:
Validation checks to flag inconsistencies or potential under-declarations, if possible
Audit trail features to support transparency, accountability, and future verification efforts

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendations that the LCE direct the City HRMO to:
Resume the use of biometric machines to ensure accurate and timely monitoring of employee attendance and to expedite the payroll process for the City.
Due to advancements in technology, consider upgrading the biometric fingerprint system to a facial recognition system for more effective and efficient monitoring of employee attendance, if the budget allows.

We recommended that the LCE instructs the City HRMO to:
Provide a copy of the office orders submitted to the Civil Service Commission (CSC) designating the concerned personnel as Acting Department Heads for specific offices.
Coordinate with the City Budget Officer to ensure proper alignment of the Plantilla schedule, ensuring that personnel are assigned to the correct positions in accordance with their designated items, provided such designation is permanent.

To enhance the accuracy and integrity of the payroll process, we recommended the LCE to transfer the responsibility for preparing payroll documents to the HRMO to ensure that payroll computations are based on the most up-to-date employee information, including employee status, compensation details, and mandatory deductions.
This also ensures proper segregation of duties between the HRMO and the Accounting Office to eliminate any self-review threats and ensure the accuracy and transparency of the payroll process.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the LCE direct the following actions:
The City Treasurer to initiate and enforce the settlement of the unpaid lease from UWM by issuing a final demand letter, in accordance with the provisions of the 2009 Rules and Regulations on the Settlement of Accounts (RRSA); and
The City Legal Officer to consider the filing of appropriate legal action against UWM should it fail to settle the obligations after the issuance of the final demand.
We further recommended that the LCE instruct the City HRMO, in coordination with the City Accountant, to withhold the salaries of the City Treasurer, identified as one of the primarily liable persons, until full settlement is made, pursuant to the instructions provided in the COE.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the City Treasurer to:
(1) Deposit the total amount of ₱1,670,598.28 (GF-City – ₱1,261,326.80; GF-UCU – ₱299.02; SEF – ₱27,506.93; TF – ₱381,465.53) into the designated fund bank accounts under the AGDB; and (2) furnish a copy of the deposit slip to the Audit Team for verification.
Create a remittance schedule for collections and issue a directive to all concerned Accountable Officers, instructing them to strictly comply with the said schedule. This is to ensure that the Liquidating Officer receives the remittances promptly and to prevent any misappropriation, particularly those collecting in other offices or on the field.
Deposit intact and daily to the AGDB all collections accumulated, regardless of fund source, through the Liquidating Officer, pursuant to Section 32 of Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended to the LCE, through the OIC-City Accountant, to adhere to the procedures for the proper disposition and derecognition of dormant accounts pursuant to Items 7.0 and 8.0 of COA Circular No. 2023-008, thus:
Secure all documents available relative to the said accounts such as accounting records, reports, vouchers, and its supporting documents.
Prepare the following schedules quarterly and submit the same on the 15th day of the ensuing month:
Schedule of Dormant Receivable Accounts
Schedule of Dormant Unliquidated Cash Advances
Schedule of Dormant Intra/Inter-agency Fund Transfers
Schedule of Other Dormant Accounts
For accounts with records and documents or when original documents are lost and destroyed, or cannot be produced, the City Accountant shall:
Prepare letters signed by the LCE addressed to each accountable officer concerned demanding the AO to execute a certificate of justification as to why the supporting documents are not available along with the narration or documentation of Management’s effort to locate such documents. In case of retired, deceased and separated AOs, the current AO shall execute the certificate of justification.
Prepare a (1) list of available records relative to the dormant account and indicate the extent of validation made thereof along with the documents itself. Include also a (2) list of lost documents or records.
Submit a detailed report together with the said documents/reports to the LCE recommending the conduct of investigation to determine the cause/s of the loss of documents and pinpoint the liable official/employee for such loss.
Further, we recommended to the LCE to:
Create an Investigation Committee to determine the cause/s of the loss or destruction of documents, pinpoint the liable official/employee, and look into the absence or presence of negligence on the part of AO through submission of an Investigation Report (IR).
Render decision-based on the IR. A copy of the IR shall be submitted to the City Accountant.
Require the City Accountant to submit a request, through the LCE, for the approval of COA to write off/derecognize from the books the dormant accounts.
Moreover, we recommended that the LCE direct the (a) City Treasurer to transfer the deposited collections from the previous years from the General Fund account to the Trust Fund account with a total amount of ₱1,864,802.96 and furnish the proof of transfer to the City Accountant. We recommended that the LCE require the OIC-City Engineer to:

Ongoing projects by the City including infrastructure projects and delivery of goods and services as of reporting date;
Actual start and target completion date of the project based on the contract agreement and other relevant documents; and
Total cost to date incurred by the City recorded in the books as of reporting date.
Status of the project if complete or ongoing; and additional remarks in case of delays or non-completion within the contract period.
(1) Prepare project signboard/billboard within the construction site visible to the public; and (2) ensure the implementation of the same by placing the project signboards/billboards within the project location before the start of any projects in compliance with the specifications provided under Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004; and
Inform the Audit Team within ten (10) days after the award or before the start of the PPA that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted pursuant to Section 3.1 of the same circular.
Utilize the geo-tagging applications to provide geographical coordinates and locations for the subject projects for a more transparent, accurate and reliable report of project accomplishments and preventing potential duplication and overlapping of infrastructure projects. All requests shall be made to the OIC- Supply Officer who shall safekeep the procured supplies for safekeeping and monitoring.

BAC Chairperson, through the BAC Secretariat, to include the purchase request accompanied by the request letter and its attachments in the request for contract review pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001.

Send out Notices of Delinquency to all delinquent taxpayers.

Provide a written explanation to the Audit Team on the granting of allowances to the Punong Barangays along with the legal basis, if any. In case the purpose of such disbursements is a donation to the Barangay, attach the Accountable Form No. 51 issued by the Barangay Treasurer concerned as an acknowledgment of receiving such amount.

observations.recommendation:
1.11. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City
Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to correct the
misstatements of the affected accounts. Henceforth, ensure that costs of
major repairs, rehabilitation and improvement of infrastructure assets
should be capitalized or recognized as PPE pursuant to IPSAS 17. The
recommended adjusting entries to be taken up in CY 2022 are as
follows:
PPE-Infrastructure Assets 3,692,998.81
PPE-Buildings and Other Structures 11,941,329.04
Prior Period Adjustments
(Government Equity) 15,634,327.85
45
1.12. We also recommended that the City Budget Officer includes all major
repairs, rehabilitations and improvements of PPE from MOOE to CO
in the Annual Budget to enable the City Accountant to record the said
disbursements to its appropriate accounts.

2.15. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Create an Inventory Team and Appraisal and Disposal
Committee that will:
 Determine the total unserviceable items together with
their carrying amount and prepare the necessary
documents to fast track the disposal of unserviceable
PPE items;
 Conduct an appraisal of the unserviceable properties
and waste materials and furnish the Audit Team a copy
of the appraisal report thereof;
 Facilitate the immediate disposal of the identified and
verified unserviceable properties and waste materials to
recoup any salvage value thereof; and
 Dispose the unserviceable properties based on the most
appropriate mode of disposal pursuant to the provisions
of Sections 379 to 381 of R.A. No. 7160 and COA Circular
Nos. 89-296 and 92-386.
b. Enjoin the OIC-Supply Officer and the GSO to prepare the
Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable
Property for the 289 identified unserviceable items and
provide the same to the City Accountant for proper
recording in the books and the Audit Team for further
verification. Also, require both officers to coordinate with
the City Accountant to determine the book value of the
remaining 160 unserviceable items without verified carrying
cost.
c. Require the City Accountant to derecognize the costs of
these properties once the disposal thereof has been
completed and the necessary documents are submitted by
the OIC-Supply Officer and the GSO.
d. Direct the OIC-Supply Officer and the GSO to provide a
written communication to the Audit Team in the event that
a government property becomes unserviceable and strictly
refrain from disposal or destruction of unserviceable items
without the presence of the Audit Team or its authorized
representative as required under Section 79 of the P.D. No.
1445.
e. Instruct the officials and employees concerned on the
disposal and destruction of the unserviceable properties to
submit a written explanation as to why the unserviceable
PPE items were not presented to the Audit Team before its
disposition.

3.8. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Assign an employee from the Supply Office or General Services
Office, to take charge of the following:
 Storekeeping of the items purchased.
 Monitoring of the actual inventory balances.
 Preparation of RIS on every issuances of inventory items.
 Preparation of weekly SSMI to be submitted, along with the
original RIS, to the City Accountant.
 Prepare the Report on Physical Count of Inventory at year
end.
b. Direct the City Budget Officer to maintain a separate
appropriation account for the recognition of expenses such as
Office Supplies expense and Welfare Goods expense through
augmentation from OMOE.
c. Instruct the City Accountant to use the perpetual inventory
system in recording purchase of supplies and materials for
issuance, consumption, utilization or distribution.

4.7. We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that:
 the Local Chief Executive directs the City Accountant to
instruct all concerned Accountable Officers to settle their cash
advances immediately as required by the provision under COA
Circular 97-002, by sending demand letters;
 the concerned officials and employees observe the provisions of
Section 4.1.2 of the same COA Circular that additional cash
advance shall not be granted unless the previous cash advances
had been liquidated or settled; and.
 Withhold the salaries or claims of employees with unliquidated
cash advances

5.10. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City
Accountant to prepare a Request for Authority to Write-Off Dormant
Receivable Accounts amounting to ₱972,271.73 to be signed by the
Head of the Agency along with the supporting documents in pursuant
to the procedures provided in Sections 8.2 and 8.3 of COA Circular No.
2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

5.11. We also recommended that the City Accountant provide appropriate
Allowance for Impairment on Other Receivables which are deemed to
be uncollectible as of the reporting date in accordance with Paragraph
67 of IPSAS 29.

6.7. We recommended that:
a. The City Accountant effect the suggested adjusting entries to reflect
the proper balance of the Accounts Payable in the financial
statements;
Accounts Payable 9,494,765.95
Government Equity 9,494,765.95
To adjust undocumented accounts payables which remained
outstanding for two years and above.
b. The City Accountant recognize Accounts Payable only when valid
and complete supporting documents are available or only at the
time goods and services are accepted or rendered and
supplier/creditor bills are received.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Management:

The Local Chief Executive directs its Legal Department to review the provisions of the existing Memorandum of Agreement between the CGU and UWM and the Contract of Lease between the CGU and Rhodenes Enterprises.

We also recommended that amendments must be made to the MoA/Contract of Lease to adjust the quantity of garbage to be dumped at the CESL, due date on monthly rental payments, and the total monthly rentals to be made, and to provide the required provision of the lessee paying deposit and advance rental payments.

Also, both parties are also invited to strictly abide through the provisions of the MoA/Contract of Lease, and should any violations of the provisions negatively affecting the CGU, initiate measures to require the private contractors fulfil their contractual obligations;

Demand settlement from the UWM and Rhodenes Enterprises of their outstanding balances of ₱3,500,000.00 and ₱300,000.00 respectively.  Also, the City Treasurer should strictly impose the City Government’s rights to charge interest for the unpaid balances and refrain from issuing official receipts to the lessees without full payment of the monthly dues.

Further, calculate the interest due from the lessees so that the City Accountant will have a basis to record the interest receivable in the books of the City Government. Moreover, the City Mayor should communicate a formal letter to the UWM and Rhodenes Enterprises reminding them to pay their outstanding balances;

Set limits on the weight of garbage disposed at the Sanitary Landfill to maximize its intended lifespan. Also, the City Government should impose penalties to the lessee dumping unreasonable quantities of garbage; and

Careful assessment and study should be made by Sangguniang Panlungsod before issuing resolutions relating to the UCESL to ensure efficiency and effectiveness of its operations. Revisit SP Resolution Nos. 338 and 339 and evaluate the current lease agreements with the private enterprises and explore other options to increase the operating income of UCESL.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Direct the personnel in-charge of the Bagsakan Public Market and the City Treasurer to regularly prepare the monthly billing statements for all stallholders and submit a summary of the delinquent stallholders for the City Accountant to book up the unpaid balances and corresponding penalties as a debit to Operating Lease Receivable and credit to Rent Income.
Instruct the City Accountant to adhere with the accrual method of accounting by properly recognizing uncollected balances from the delinquent stallholders, as prescribed under Paragraph 19 of IPSAS No. 9 and pertinent provisions of GAM for LGUs.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that Management direct the City Treasurer, in coordination with the City Accountant to reconcile the treasury report with the subsidiary records to reflect the correct amount of the subject receivables.
We also reiterated that Management require the City Assessor to assist the City Treasurer in the preparation of a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card which will provide an accurate and reliable basis in the setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes.
The City Accountant pointed out that the late submission of information from the City Treasurer caused the difference in the recorded balance of RPT/SET Receivables. In response, the City Treasurer agreed to provide the needed data by the Accounting Office so that the same will not happen in the next accounting period.
On the non-reconciliation of the year-end balance of the Real Property Tax Receivable, the City Accountant stated that he had coordinated with the Treasury Office and found out that the latter also failed to note the balance of the uncollected amount for CY 2020. The Treasury Office is still verifying the information and will inform the City Accountant with a report on the receivables as basis for recording in the books.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to record future purchase of all inventoriable items as Inventory as defined under Paragraph 11 of IPSAS 12 – Inventories and recognize these items as expenses upon consumption, distribution or utilization for proper recording in the books of account.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Management direct the City Accountant, Supply Officer and other concerned department heads to:
Conduct the physical count of PPE and reconcile the actual result with the accounting records to provide accurate, updated and complete records of PPE; and
Prepare and submit the RPCPPE not later than January 31 of each year, pursuant to Section 266 of GAM for LGUs, Volume I.
We also recommended that Management require the City Accountant prepare and maintain subsidiary ledger cards for the various PPE items and prepare the necessary adjusting entries for the PPE items recorded as expenses and provide depreciation for the land used for the sanitary landfill operations.

We recommended that Management, instruct the Local Finance Committee to provide the supplemental budget, approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod, for the augmentation of the Bayanihan grant budget appropriation from MOOE to Capital Outlay covering the amount spent and to be spent, and furnish a copy of the same to this office.
We also recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to record necessary adjusting journal entries for the recognition of capital expenditures incurred as PPE and to recognize depreciation for the related PPE as provided under IPSAS 17 – PPE for proper recording in the books of accounts.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to observe the provisions of the GAM for LGUs in recording assets to avoid overstatement of the PPE presented in the financial statements at year-end, such as only assets delivered with government ownership and control should be recorded in the books.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Treasurer to secure the TCT of the said lots acquired in Barangays Poblacion and Anonas including the other lacking documents to ensure ownership of land amounting to ₱22,500,000.00 and to fully protect the interest of the City in compliance with Section 39 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers for each LEEs and PU and submit the trial balance and financial statements for all special accounts of the LGU as part of schedules of the General Fund in consonance with the Section 313 of RA No. 7160 and Sections 141, 142 and 143 of the GAM for LGUs, Volume II.
We also recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to open a separate bank account for the existing economic enterprise of the LGU through a resolution from the Sangguniang Panlungsod.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the City Treasurer to secure all undeposited cash collections, cash items and accountable forms with and without money value in a fire-proof secured vault or safe deposit boxes to minimize the risks of government loss through natural and/or man-made occurrences.
Direct all accountable officers with collection functions to maintain an updated daily record on their respective cashbooks to properly maintain its records.
Require the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to reconcile accounting and treasury records every month to come up with an adjusted cashbook balance and GL/SL balance and refrain from allowing unreconciled balances for longer periods of time wherein reconciliation of such would be difficult.
Require the City Treasurer to secure fidelity bond for all accountable officers. In case of market collections wherein job-order employees were designated as accountable officers, have them remit their collections with the market supervisor, since he had bond and have him remit the same to the Treasurer’s Office.
Direct the City Treasurer to adopt the use of Requisition Issue Voucher for proper record keeping of the accountable forms issued and remind collecting officers to refrain from borrowing cash tickets from one another.
Require the City Treasurer to prepare and submit RAAF monthly to the Audit Team for proper monitoring and reconciliation of the accountable forms.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management direct the Accountable Officers to settle their cash advances immediately as required by the provision on COA Circular 97-002, by sending demand letters. We recommended that Management stop engaging the services of consultants without undergoing the procedures outlined in the Revised IRR of the RA No. 9184 and pertinent budgeting, accounting and auditing rules and regulations. Also, engagement of these consultants should have projects that were not a regular function of the agency or not made to perform functions which are part of the job description of the agency’s existing regular employees.

We recommended that City Government require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to create a library or database of price and items lists with the help from the different departments/offices which will be used in the determination of a realistic and reasonable Approved Budget of Contract for the procurement process.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to submit the lacking financial documents and all other accounts subject for audit as required under Section 122 of PD 1445 to prevent delay on the part of the Audit Team in conducting timely audit on the subject reports and document. We also recommended that Management implement routing slip on disbursement vouchers to facilitate monitoring, checking and detecting on areas where the bottleneck exists in the process flow.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Officer-in-Charge of the UCESL to conduct a feasibility study on the UCESL operations whether to continue the lease agreements with UWM and RE, as the existing contracts are disadvantageous to the City Government.
Also, Management must have a feasibility study for future contracts with similar nature first before entering thereto to ensure that the most advantageous action will be taken.
Consider making amendments to the MoA/Contract of Lease to adjust the quantity of garbage to be dumped at the UCESL or have the SP of the City of Urdaneta pass a resolution to set limitation on the waste disposal to lengthen the useful life of the sanitary landfill.
Require the lessees to strictly abide with the provisions of the MoA/Contract of Lease, and should any violations of the provisions negatively affecting the City Government, initiate measures to require the private contractors fulfill their contractual obligations.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that Management exert all measures to collect the outstanding balance by having the City Treasurer issue a demand letter to all delinquent taxpayer and if they failed to settle their delinquency, avail remedies as vested under Section 254 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and to avoid penalties or sanctions provided by Section 259 of the same Code.

We recommended that Management direct the University Supply Officer to distribute first the unutilized inventories from prior periods, especially those items which may not last after a year before the recent procured inventories to prevent wastage of inventory stocks for an efficient and effective operation.

We recommended that Management require the CSWDO to furnish a certified list of affected families/households as supporting document to the disbursement vouchers for the relief operations. A summary or list of distributed goods and report on remaining inventory should also be prepared and submitted by the concerned officer in charge of the distribution to complete documentation of the transactions pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445.
We recommended that Management secure documents certifying that the nearest relative of the deceased PUI is not financially capable of defraying the expenses for the burial, such as certificate of indigency to support the claims.
We recommended that Management do the following:
Furnish copies of the monthly required reports such as the Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Implementation of PPAs related to the Bayanihan Grant to the Audit Team for verification.
Instruct the MIS Department to post the required reports in the official website of the LGU at the end of the month.
Evaluate the possibility of continuing or discontinuing the maintenance of the official website of the LGU, to ensure that government resources are effectively and efficiently utilized.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that Management require the CDRRMO chief to coordinate with different offices and prepare and submit the required reports and strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 and furnish the same to the NDRRMC through the Office of the Civil Defense (OCD), and copy furnished the respective COA Auditor.
We also recommended that Management requires that the Information and Communications Technology (ICT) department to update the website with the reports on the LDRRM for public information.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Management require the City Accounting Office and City Budget Office to submit promptly the data needed by the GAD Focal Person for the former to accomplish and furnish the GAD accomplishment report.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the City Government must conduct a physical count of PPE items and prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant & Equipment (RPCPPE) not later than January 31 of each year, pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs.
In addition, the City Accounting Office should maintain Property/Equipment ledger cards which indicated therein the complete description of every specific PPE including its corresponding number. Likewise, the Supply Officer, as property custodian, should provide and complete the inventory tag or markings on every specific PPE to have ready reference in determining whether PPE found and located in specific office or area are the same with those recorded in the books. Ensure proper accomplishment of the ARE and ICS to monitor the issuance, transfer or turnover of properties from accountable officers to the new end-users.
It was further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accounting Office and the Supply Officer to give priority to the reconciliation of the recorded PPE with an actual physical count of the PPE items to provide accurate, updated and complete records of PPE.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive shall:
Require the General Services Office to periodically render Report on Local Road Networks, and furnish copies to the concerned offices. Also, City Engineer’s Office should provide the City Accountant and the General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
Create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count of public infrastructures, specially the local road networks.
Direct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to properly take up the unrecorded road lots. Maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for each individual road networks.
Ensure coordination among LGU Offices to properly implement the accounting and reporting guidelines of COA Circular 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

We recommended that the City Accountant be directed to determine those projects which are still lodged in the CIP account. It was also recommended that the necessary coordination be made with the City Engineer’s Office as regards the status of the projects, in order that all completed projects could be properly classified and a more accurate account balance of the CIP account could be presented in the financial statements.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendation that the Accounting Office and Treasury Office coordinate closely and make proper adjustments on the subject receivables in order that the accounting records reconciles with the treasury records to reflect a more reliable and accurate amount of receivables.
Likewise, the City Treasurer in coordination with the City Assessor, prepare and furnish the City Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers showing the names and amount due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card to provide an accurate and reliable basis in the setting-up of receivables for real property taxes and special education taxes for fair presentation of the account in the financial statements and pursuant to the provision of afore-cited regulations.

We reiterated our prior year audit recommendation that City Accountant analyze and reconcile the balances of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds and effect the necessary adjusting entries for proper presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended that the necessary correcting entries be made in the books relative to the purchase of cattle and frozen semen, and including its distribution thereof. The following are the suggested entries:
(1) Dr. Biological Assets 			P1,170,000.00
Cr. 	Other Maintenance
and Operating Exp.			P1,170,000.00
To recognize the biological assets in the books
(2) Dr. Due to NGAs				P1,170,000.00
Cr.	Biological Assets			P1,170,000.00
To record the distribution of the biological assets to recipients.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the management deposit the collection daily and intact pursuant to the provision of Section 32 of the MNGAS to safeguard the fund from its possible misuse.

We recommended that Management adopt proper segregation of duties; incompatible functions must be performed by different employees, to avoid any individual to have inordinate control over transactions and resources. Authorization for cancellation of official receipts must be delegated as a management function of the agency.

We recommended that Management require that the submitted report of collections and deposits show the various nature of collections to inform users of the information on the actual daily collections made to properly present financial information to various stakeholders.

It was recommended that Management direct the Accountable Officers to settle their cash advances immediately as required by the provision on COA Circular 97-002, by sending demand letters. The City Accountant shall observe the provisions of Section 4.1.2 of the same COA Circular that additional cash advance shall not be granted unless the previous cash advances had been liquidated or settled.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive make a follow up from the Sangguniang Panlungsod for an authority for the reversion of long outstanding and undocumented payables pursuant to the provision of Section 98 of Presidential Decree 1445 and to make use of the idle fund reserved for such payables. Compliance of Management will be duly validated.

We recommended that management expedite the hiring process of new employees by filling up the vacancies noted as soon as possible so that the University could improve its service and program delivery to the public and provide job equal opportunities to the public.
It was further recommended that UCU designate a focal person who will mobilize plan of actions to provide sound decision-making in managing its manpower resources.
We also recommended strict adherence with the provisions of Section 2 of RA 7041 and Section 29, Rule VII of the 2017 Omnibus Rules on Appointments and Other Human Resource Actions (ORAOHRA).

We recommended that Management ensure strict compliance with Section 350 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and Section 4.1 of P.D. 1445 by requiring that all prior year transactions be properly obligated before payment thereof. Comply with the modified accrual basis of accounting for complete reporting of all financial transactions.  Obligate all valid claims not paid during the year for fair presentation of the financial statements.
We also recommended that Management submit an explanation why said transactions amounting to P1,628,474.82 were paid using the current appropriation.

We recommended that Members of Sangguniang Panlungsod stop appropriating financial assistance under its office considering that it is not a legislative function. Any funds that maybe set aside for the welfare of the people shall be lodged to offices under the Local Chief Executive.

We recommended that Management:
Execute Memorandum of Agreement with the intended school beneficiaries to ensure that government funds will be utilized for public purpose and the recipients will be fully accountable and responsible for the program/project/activity implementation. Submit the lacking liquidation reports and require the succeeding beneficiaries to submit the same upon utilization of the financial assistance.
Formulate written policies and guidelines on the financial assistance or donations given to ensure proper disposition of government funds and maximize the effectiveness and efficiency of government operations.

From the deficiencies noted we recommended to the management the following:
City Social Welfare and Development Office (CSWDO) submit the lacking supporting documents, namely the Microenterprise Plan and Training Design relating to the project, as well as the Memorandum of Agreement between the LGU and project beneficiaries. We also recommended that recipients are required to have photocopies of their identification card and submit to this Office.
All concerned offices must be directed to submit Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption per vehicle supported with approved and properly accomplished trip tickets, monthly report of travels undertaken (per vehicle), daily gas issue record, and fuel control card of logbook (record of gasoline/fuel issuance per vehicle). Likewise, officials who are receiving their Travelling Allowance and yet they were issued gasoline, be required to refund the amount corresponding to the cost of gasoline pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular 77-61 and Section 4(6) of P.D. 1445.
Submit the list of guests duly signed by the recipients and the schedule of accreditation for different programs to support claims on the accommodation expenses.
Strictly observe the provisions of Section 4(6) of PD 1445 by not allowing payment for claims with incomplete supporting papers. Follow the correct procedures in the disbursement of government funds specifically on the financial assistance made through the petty cash fund by ensuring complete documentation and proper official signatories.

We recommended that management set-up the 5% share of DPWH in a trust fund and 15% share of the Office of the Building Official (OBO) upon collection of the building permit fees so that the City Treasurer can regularly remit the said funds to the Bureau of Treasury. The inability to do the same may subject the City Treasurer to the sanction provided under paragraph h, item 2 of Section 210 of the IRR.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod shall have careful assessment and study on the operations of the Engineered Sanitary Landfill before issuing resolution and granting authorization to the Local Chief Executive to ensure its effectiveness and efficiencies.
We further recommended that Management set limits on weight of garbage disposed at the Engineered Sanitary Landfill to maximize its intended lifespan. Furthermore, Management should consider evaluating the current agreements with private enterprises and explore other options to increase its operating income. Also, utilization of government resources should not be shared with the lessees and make them adhere to the provisions of lease agreements.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 and through the CDRRMO chief, initiate the timely preparation and submission of the required reports and furnish the same to the NDRRMC through the Office of the Civil Defense (OCD), on the 5th day following the end of each month, and copy furnished the respective COA Auditor. Moreover, have the Information and Communications Technology (ICT) department to update the website with the reports on the LDRRM for public information.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the members of the Sangguniang Panlungsod make a further review of the terms and conditions of the contract of MMSCI and make the necessary amendments of the provisions which seemingly disadvantageous to the government and may put the LGU in a legal bind.

We reiterate our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Acting City Treasurer sent monthly billing and demand letters when the stallholders become delinquent for at least three months to make stallholders aware of their obligations to settle their monthly rentals. Institute legal action against the delinquent stallholders who refuse to settle their past due accounts to improve the City’s collection efficiency and to accumulate funds for the implementation of more beneficial programs and projects. Compliance will be followed up and validated.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the Acting City Treasurer shall issue a demand letter to all delinquent taxpayer and after they failed to settle their delinquency, avail remedies as vested under Section 254 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and to avoid penalties or sanctions provided by Section 259 of the same Code.

We recommended that UCU management stop granting honoraria to university officials and personnel for handling different committees which are deemed part of regular agency functions and mandate. We also recommended the refund of the amount of P453,000.00 which is not within a specific activity or project contemplated under the Local Budget Circular No. 62.

We recommended that Management utilize the benefits of the installed timekeeping system and provide employees with a print-out copy of their DTRs generated by the system. Ensure validity and completeness of the supporting documents to the payroll vouchers by properly attaching these DTRs and avoid processing of salaries and honoraria payment without complete documentation.
We also recommended that Management submit the lacking print-out copies of the DTRs of the employees for the period January – December 2018 pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD 1445 to avoid suspensions in audit which will mature into audit disallowances if not settled within 90 days.
We further recommended that employees’ leave credits be provided to the Audit Team pursuant to the provisions of Section 122 of PD 1445.

We recommended the City Accountant, City Treasurer and the HRMO to initiate the necessary controls on the salary deductions of the municipal officials and employees by ensuring that their monthly net take home pay shall not reduce to an amount lower than Five Thousand Pesos (P5,000.00) in pursuance of Section 48 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act, FY 2018. Should there be a deficiency in loan repayments as a result of the increased mandatory net pay, concerned officials and employees shall coordinate with the lending associations for a direct payment arrangement.

We recommended that Management facilitate the preparation and submission of the GAD Accomplishment Report within five (5) working days from the end of January of the preceding audit year pursuant to the provisions of Section V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 28, 2014 and Section 122 of PD 1445. We also recommended the utilization of the HGDG tool as required in Section C.4 of the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA in the attribution of its programs, projects and activities to the GAD Plan and Budget. Ensure that all PPAs are in line with the objectives of the gender issue being addressed and refrain from including expenses that cannot be charged to the GAD budget.

The UCU President should submit to the City Accountant the documents that support the acquisition of the aforementioned Property, Plant and Equipment for its recognition in the books of accounts pursuant to the provision of Section 63 of PD 1445 and for better control in its custodianship and utilization.

We recommended that the City Accountant Office to comply with the afore-cited regulations which requires the maintenance of special accounts with the complete subsidiary records for the transactions of the UCU, Sanitary Landfill, and Market and Slaughterhouse as bases in the preparation of their financial statements which are essential for decision-making and future reference and in pursuant to the provision of Section 105 to 110 of the MNGAS.

We reiterated the previous year recommendation that the City Treasurer sent monthly billing and demand letters when the stallholders become delinquent for at least three months. Institute legal action against the delinquent stallholders who refuse to settle their past due accounts to improve its collection efficiency and to accumulate funds for the implementation of more beneficial programs and projects.

We recommended the immediate implementation of the program so that corresponding liquidation/fund utilization report could be prepared and submitted the same to the DSWD to avoid the delay of the release of additional fund for the program. This would ensure the continuance of the program which may result to the achievement of the goals of the government that is to make every Filipino children grow healthy through provision of proper nutrition.

We have reiterated our previous audit recommendation that the management continue tracking down the location of the former accountable officer in order to enforce the settlement of the said Notice of Charge as mandated under Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

Reconcile the general ledger controlling account balance with the listing of the General Services Office and the City Assessor’s Office in order to identify the discrepancies committed and consequently prepare the necessary adjustments. 

The City Accountant to coordinate with the City GSO, City Assessor and the City Legal Officer on the proper documentation and inventory of the real properties owned by the City. 

Facilitate the transfer of the titles of the land which are still in the name of former owners/donors/vendors to the City Government to avoid third party claims. 

The City Accountant and the City GSO to maintain updated real property ledger cards to effectively control and monitor any movement in the Land Account in order to present in fairly the financial position of the City.
observations.managementAction:
2015AAR | The Local Chief Executive created an appraisal committee to appraise PPE acquired through donations, considering that the management believes that the discrepancies between the ledger balances with the listing of the GSO were those acquired without cost.

He also assigned the legal officer to act on the registration of the land owned by the City, hence, the titling in the process.

The City Assessor had submitted the inventory of land to the Accountant’s Office which may be served as basis in recording of the same in the books of accounts.

However, balances of the account remained unreconciled.

observations.recommendation:
Require all accountable officers and employees with outstanding cash advances to immediately settle and/or liquidate their respective accountability, pursuant to COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 89 of PD 1445. 

In the event that the officials and employees concerned fail to settle/liquidate their cash advances as of January 31, 2016, file an appropriate Malversation charges against them under Articles 217 and Article 218 for failure to liquidate the said cash advance, both under the Revised Penal Code or criminal prosecution under Section 128 of P.D. No. 1445 as provided under Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2012-004, dated November 28, 2012.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR | The City Accountant issued Demand letter, hence, Out of P9,270,679.42 unliquidated cash advance as of December 31, 2015, the amount of P7,244,453.46 were liquidated. However, as of December 31, 2016, the unliquidated cash advance still have huge balance of P7,757,905.78 which included P2,026,225.96 that pertained to previous year.

observations.recommendation:
Require the City Accountant and the LDRRMO to comply with the preparation and submission of the monthly sources and utilization of the LDRRMF in compliance with the Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
Require the City Accountant to record all the unexpended balances of the two disaster related funds to the  Trust Fund books in accordance with the above cited recommended accounting entries as provided by COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction: 2015AAR | The unexpended LDRM Fund as of December 31, 2015 was transferred to Trust Fund on October 2016 for the City Proper Account and November 2016 for the Urdaneta City University.

observations.recommendation: The City Treasurer be instructed to immediately return the unused amount to the source agency all excess or unused funds so that these may be utilized in other important and urgent projects. We also recommended the City Treasurer to remit the unexpended balances of PDAF to the National Treasury together with the earned interests that have accrued to the principal amount that is currently maintained in the bank.
observations.managementAction: 2015AAR | The unexpended PDAF was remitted to the Bureau of Treasury as September 2016.

observations.recommendation: Utilize Special Education Fund exclusively for purposes for which it was created as embodied in the RA 5447 and Section 272 of RA 7160 in order to promote quality education in every public schools.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR | The management justified that the expenditure out of SEF were in accordance with the Budget approved by the school/board.

observations.recommendation: Submit the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval in consonance with the provision of paragraph 2 of PCW-DILG Joint Circular No. 2014-01. Full implementation of GAD Fund is also recommended in order to realize the intended result and extending the expected benefits to beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: 2015 AAR | The GAD Plans and Budget was submitted to DILG and to the Provincial Planning and Development Office.

observations.recommendation: The City Treasurer  sent monthly billing and demand letters when the stallholders become delinquent for at least three months to ensure collection of past due stall rentals. Institute legal action against the delinquent stall owners who refuse to settle their past due accounts. Likewise, observe proper administration of vacant stalls and post in the vacant stall “available for lease”. Do not let other stallholders to display their product on the vacant stall. Find ways to generate income by offering them to interested parties. Take appropriate action to maintain and protect them from illegal occupants.
observations.managementAction: 2015AAR | The City Treasurer issued a demand letters to the delinquent stall holders. Likewise, the management adopted a policy that delinquent shall not be issued with permit and licenses. However, as of year-end, the amount of                 P 12,590,266.00 were not collected.

observations.recommendation: Exert more efforts in the collection of taxes due and collectible by sending RPTOP’s and notice of delinquency to taxpayers and forming committee to conduct an information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes. Comply with the provision embodied in the Local Government Code of 1991 in the collections of delinquent taxes.
observations.managementAction: 2015AAR | Demand letters were issued to delinquent taxpayers, thus, there were reduction of delinquent Real Property Taxes to P10,493,145.30
